channel.title: maGaming RSS Feed - www.thesixthaxis.com
channel.description: A cleaned-up version of the original gaming feed for www.thesixthaxis.com

item.title: Ardem public playtest is coming in August
item.content:encoded:
Techtive Games has made some major announcements about its own future and its upcoming open world survival RPG Ardem, including an expansion of the team and the confirmed target for a public playtest. First off, the public playtest is scheduled for August 31st, allowing the team to spend time refining the experience so Ardem is a smooth experience when the playtest launches. Techtive Games has secured additional funding which is allowing the team to be expanded to 15 full time developers, a small welcome in the current environment.

Although a public playtest has been confirmed for Ardem, Techtive Games has also announced that there will be smaller, closed testing which will be under NDA so nothing can be shared from those. Those will be taking place over the next couple of weeks with the focus being on bug finding and level polishing. Testers are encouraged to try and break the game as much as possible during these playtests for Ardem, so the development team can fix them. A new website for Ardem is being developed too which will be the home for all future updates, including the official Ardem road map.

Ardem is a survival game set years after the collapse of civilisation which came around due to a fast spreading virus, which turned most people into mutated beings filled with hunger and rage. Players can go it alone or team up with others to scavenge for items and rebuild small points of civilisation within a 64 km² map that has been crafted by the devs, with hundreds of points of interest. Online servers will host up to 64 players per world, with player actions permanently altering the server world. There will be plenty of dangers to face with various enemy types, along with a battle to survive as you will need to look for or grow food. The weather and temperature will also impact your survival as you will need to dress correctly to face the changing environment.

Source: Steam



https://www.thesixthaxis.com/2026/03/21/ardem-public-playtest-is-coming-in-august/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Something for the Weekend – 21/03/26
item.content:encoded:
It’s been a funny old week where some of the most exciting news stories have been about console firmware updates. Backward compatibility can now be much better on Switch 2, PS5 Pro owners can get better PSSR upscaling… but then there’s the absolutely bizarre push from Nvidia with DLSS 5 to have Instagram or TikTok AI filters slathered across your games.

So, with all that and more, lets get to the news.

In the News This Week

  • Starfield is coming to PS5 in April – and there’s the huge Free Lanes and Terran Armada coming too
  • Sony to retire the PSN branding
  • Switch 2 System Update 22.0.0 adds Handheld Boost Mode to play Switch 1 games at higher resolution
  • A PS5 system update has brought upgraded PSSR to Silent Hill 2, Alan Wake 2 and more
  • DLSS 5 revealed and it’s getting a kicking as it looks like an AI filter but Nvidia CEO says critics are “completely wrong”*
  • Dark Scrolls will slowly roll onto Switch and PC this year, always moving from left to right
  • Double Fine throws down and fires up a Kiln release date for April
  • LEGO Batman: Legacy of the Dark Knight gets an earlier release date
  • FBC: Firebreak gets its final content update, a lower price and Friends Pass

*Critics probably say that he is completely wrong.

Support TheSixthAxis with Ko-fi

With the current landscape of the games media, we’re exploring more direct reader-funding so we can continue to invest in the site, and support our writers, allowing them to continue to pen the news, features, and reviews that you visit us for. To that end, we have launched a Ko-fi page earlier this year.

Support TheSixthAxis on Ko-fi

Games in Review & Featured Articles

One of the biggest games of the year is already upon us with the much anticipated open world RPG action of Crimson Desert. We’re not pinning a score on it just yet, but Dom’s spent a lot of time sinking into its vast world. You can read his review in progress here, and check in on PS5 performance here.

For the rest of the reviews we had:

  • Diablo 2: Reign of the Warlock – DLC – 8/10
  • Dragonkin: The Banished – PS5, XSX|S, PC – 7/10
  • Resident Evil Village Switch 2 – NSW2 – 6/10
  • Starship Troopers Ultimate Bug War – PS5, XSX|S, NSW2, PC – 5/10

Jason then brought us an early access review for Solasta 2, which is coming along quite well.

Elsewhere, I got to go hands on with Pragmata for a new section of the game, and came away with a clearer idea than ever what this sci-fi hack & shooter game is going to play like. I’m looking forward to this one!

With the release of Avowed on PS5 recently, Jim opined that it’s making the long wait for The Elder Scrolls 6 a little easier to handle, while Aran previewed the humorous point and click adventuring of The Darkside Detective: Backside of the Moon.

Rounding out the week, What We Played featured Crimson Desert, Marathon & Towerborne.

Trailer Park

Vampire Crawlers emerged on 21st April

Shenmue 3: Enhanced Edition revealed

The Angry Birds are coming to Two Point Museum

That’s all for the round up this week. Enjoy the weekend and we’ll carry on carrying on on Monday.



https://www.thesixthaxis.com/2026/03/21/something-for-the-weekend-21-03-26/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot

item.title: Death Stranding 2: On The Beach feels great on the PC (but not so great on the Steam Deck)
item.content:encoded:
Death Stranding 2: On The Beach is a PlayStation 5 games that truly looks and feels like a PlayStation 5 game. This has been a generation with games that have often felt trapped between souping up the last generation of graphics, and coming to terms with console hardware that really struggles to handle all the ray-tracing, lighting and upscaling demands that will be key to the next. Death Stranding 2, though, had my jaw dropping in it’s opening moments at how gorgeous, photo-realistic, and breath-taking it looked – and in all my hours of playing the game, that feeling somehow never went away. Getting to experience that again with this PC port is a delight, although the second time around and on now somewhat older hardware, the cracks do begin to show.

For reference, I’m running a PC with a GeForce RTX 3080, a Ryzen 5 5600X, and 32GB of RAM, which all comes just a hair shy of the full recommended PC specs for the game provided by Kojima Productions and PlayStation. At first glance, the game runs like a dream. It’s a perfect blend of the “Performance” and “Quality” modes from the original PS5 release, providing a 1440P experience at a smooth 60FPS with plenty of room to hit higher frames if left uncapped. Running at full 4K is a bit trickier on this hardware, requiring me to really fiddle with the more granular graphics settings to land on a consistent framerate. The PC port provides a host of new frame-generation options that help add fluidity to my 4K experience, despite my lack of supercomputer-tier hardware.

I chose to keep the game at 1440P and 60FPS, though, opting for an experience that didn’t rely on frame-gen. What I noticed, though, was that even on this seemingly stable setup, I was regularly encountering some minor stutters and frame-drops throughout the game. It never fully went away no matter how long I played, and these stutters were never present on the original PS5 version of the game, which is a frustrating knock against this PC port.

As a diehard fan of the Steam Deck, I simply had to try this game out on my OLED Deck to see how it performed. I knew it was impossible to expect a 1:1 experience on the aged hardware of Valve’s hybrid handheld, but I’ve also been surprised in the past by how competently some of these Sony PC ports can run on the Steam Deck if you really crank down the settings – The Last Of Us 2 was a good example of this. Unfortunately, despite even going so far as to offer a dedicated “Portable” graphics preset in-game, Death Stranding 2 is a mess on the handheld console.

Framerate inconsistently bounces around the 15-25FPS range at all times, and only really reaches 30FPS if you’re indoors and are feeling lucky. Again, I’m not surprised that a game of this scale can’t run at all on the Steam Deck, but it’s always a fun experiment to turn settings down to their minimum and see how these kinds of games fare.

Further Reading: Death Stranding 2 Review

Overall, Death Stranding 2 on PC is a mostly stellar experience, but the constant threat of minor frame drops and stutters at seemingly random times prevent this from feeling as seamless or polished as it did on the original hardware. It’s absolutely a solid enough port for PC-only Kojima fans to finally dive into his latest epic, but hopefully in time the rough patches get ironed out so this can become the perfect way to play this game.



https://www.thesixthaxis.com/2026/03/20/death-stranding-2-on-the-beach-feels-great-on-the-pc-but-not-so-great-on-the-steam-deck/
item.dc:creator: invalid@example.com (ResetEra_Newsbot
